Portuguese.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' PORT 3130 Placement' or PORT 2030 .

Portuguese.name: PORT 3150 Summer in Brazil: Immersive Grammar & Writing II (4)
Portuguese.description: This course is designed as a summer abroad option for students with the equivalent of three or more semesters of previous Portuguese language study. Students will expand their lexicon through engagement with texts written for native-speaking audiences. Meanwhile, they will enhance the accuracy and confidence of their spoken language through the production of sophisticated analysis and discussion. The course applies a communicative learning approach to provide students with broad skills to communicate in natural language contexts.

Portuguese.name: PORT 3190 Brazilian Short Stories (3)
Portuguese.description: This course provides an introduction to the Brazilian short story from 1870 to the present, while providing intermediate to advanced training in Portuguese conversation and composition.

Portuguese.name: PORT 3250 Composition & Convers (3)
Portuguese.description: Reinforcement of spoken Portuguese and review of grammatical structures. Short stories and plays serve as the basis for further development of speaking and writing. Emphasis in dealing with the texts is on their utility for skill practice rather than literary analysis.
Portuguese.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' PORT 3250 Placement' or PORT 2030 .

Portuguese.name: PORT 3280 Advanced Portuguese through Brazilian Film (3)
Portuguese.description: Through a series of film viewings, readings, and access to other visual media from Brazil, students receive instruction in how to discuss and analyze visual culture in Portuguese. Vocabulary building and strategies for enhanced viewing and reading comprehension are stressed. Significant emphasis on the continued development of linguistic skills.

Portuguese.name: PORT 3290 Special Topics (3)
Portuguese.description:
Course will expand upon grammar and vocabulary learned in 1120-2030 sequence. Emphasis on written and oral production in specific registers. Possible themes include
 
Portuguese across the Lusophone world, regional studies in the Lusophone world, professional skills, historical development of Portuguese, Portuguese pronunciation. The precise topic varies from year to year.
Course may be repeated 3 times for credit.
Portuguese.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): PORT 2030 or minimum score of PASS in ' PORT 3290 Placement'.

Portuguese.name: PORT 3330 Brazilian Lit Translatn (3)
Portuguese.description: A survey of Brazilian literature in translation, focusing primarily on the novel and short story. Students engage a wide variety of texts, including representative works of romanticism, realism, modernism and postmodernism. This course may be taken for major or minor credit if written work is completed in Portuguese.

Portuguese.name: PORT 3340 Brazilian Women Writers (3)
Portuguese.description: An introductory survey of influential Brazilian women writers of prose fiction, with a focus on literary treatment of questions of gender, sexuality, race, and class.

Portuguese.name: PORT 3890 Service Learning (0-1)
Portuguese.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4100 Gender & Sexuality Brazillian (3)
Portuguese.description: This course proposes a historicized and interdisciplinary consideration of gender and sexuality in modern Brazil through short fiction, films, documentaries, popular music, and critical texts. It will address a wide range of topics, including patriarchal power and the construction of masculinity, the quest for female subjectivity, gender in relation to race and class, the constitution and crisis of the bourgeois family, marital strife and infidelity, homosexuality, and transgender performance.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4110 Race and Ethnicity in Brazilian Literature and Culture (3)
Portuguese.description: This course will focus on the construction of “race” as a social category and ethnicity as a process of cultural distinction in relation to Brazilian nationality. While making use of historical and, to a limit extent, social scientific materials, our focus will be on literary texts and other cultural artifacts dating from the mid-nineteenth century to the present. We will engage canonical texts of Brazilian literature, as well as recent works.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4120 Social Problems in Brazilian Literature & Culture (3)
Portuguese.description: The chief problems of Brazilian society as reflected in fiction, testimony, poetry, theatre, music, and other forms of cultural expression. Representative works may concern persistent race, class, and gender inequalities; tyranny and political repression; violence; and/or environmental issues.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4130 Topics in Brazilian Literature (3)
Portuguese.description:
Readings in Brazilian stories, essays, and poems, focusing on a topic of historical and cultural importance. Some themes: women in Brazilian literature, regionalism, Afro-Brazilian culture, soccer. The precise topic varies from year to year.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.
Portuguese.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): PORT 3130 , 3250 , 3280 or minimum score of PASS in 'PORT 4000 Level Placement'.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4160 Afro-Brazilians (3)
Portuguese.description: This course provides an introduction to the history of Brazilian race relations, the fiction and poetry of black writers from Brazil, and the study of recent Afro-Brazilian cultural and social movements.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4290 Brazilian Cultural Study (3)
Portuguese.description: A survey of Brazilian cultural practices and discourses of the twentieth century that engages historic and contemporary debates in Brazil surrounding nationality, modernity, democracy, and citizenship.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4440 Brazilian Popular Music (3)
Portuguese.description: This course examines Brazilian cultural history through the prism of popular music, often regarded as Brazil's most accomplished field of artistic production. The study of music will provide the basis for the exploration of issues such as nationalism, regionalism, developmentalism, authoritarianism, and globalization.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4510 Luzo-Brazilian Cities (3)
Portuguese.description: An advanced undergraduate course with a focus on the literary and cultural production of a major city of the Portuguese-speaking world including Lisbon, Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Salvador da Bahia, Luanda, and Maputo. Course may be repeated 2 times for credit.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4610 Brazilian Cinema (3)
Portuguese.description: This survey of Brazilian cinema and film criticism covers key phases in national film production including early experiments, the failed Vera Cruz enterprise, Cinema Novo, Cinema Marginal, Embrafilme productions, and recent film directors include Mário Peixoto, Humberto Mauro, Anselmo Duarte, Nelson Pereira dos Santos, Ruy Guerra, Glauber Rocha, Carlos Diegues, Walter Lima Junior, Luiz Carlos Barreto, Paulo César Saraceni, Joaquim Pedro de Andrade, Rogério Sganzerla, Júlio Bressane, Suzana Amaral, and Carla Camurati.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4910 Independent Study (3)
Portuguese.description:
PORT 3000-level sequence and departmental approval.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Portuguese.name: PORT 4990 Honors Thesis (3)
Portuguese.description: For especially qualified seniors with approval of the faculty director. Students are generally expected to have a minimum of a 3.400 overall grade-point average and a 3.500 grade-point average in the major.

Portuguese.name: PORT 5000 Honors Thesis (4)
Portuguese.description: For especially qualified seniors with approval of the faculty director. Students are generally expected to have a minimum of a 3.400 overall grade-point average and a 3.500 grade-point average in the major.

Portuguese.name: PORT 5380 Junior Year Abroad (1-20)
Portuguese.description:
Junior Year Abroad.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Portuguese.name: PORT 5390 Junior Year Abroad (1-20)
Portuguese.description: Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6000 Independent Study (1-3)
Portuguese.description: Independent Study.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6130 Brazilian Cultural Imaginaries (3)
Portuguese.description: This course is a textual and experiential exploration of Brazil and specifically the city of São Paulo as part of the Tulane Summer in Brazil program.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6150 Contemporary Social Issues in Brazil - Inequalities in Brazil (4)
Portuguese.description: This course, part of the Tulane Summer in Brazil Program, introduces Brazilian culture and contemporary social issues. The first section examines: national identity, Brazil’s colonial past and its consequences, the struggles and resistance of indigenous peoples, quilombola people and other “populações tradicionais” and race. The second section will discuss violence and resistance in Brazil, race, gender, labor, urbanization and the struggle for civil representation and decent housing. The last section will discuss social policies in Brazil, with a focus on the National Health System and intersectional inequalities based on race, gender, migration and refugees in Brazil.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6160 Afro-Brazilians (3)
Portuguese.description: This course provides an introduction to the history of Brazilian race relations, the fiction and poetry of black writers from Brazil, and the study of recent Afro-Brazilian cultural and social movements.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6190 Avant-Garde Move Lat Am (3)
Portuguese.description: This course surveys the avant-garde movements in Spanish America and Brazil, focusing on the period from 1916 to 1935. Some of the movements to be examined include Huidobro's creacionismo, ultraísmo, Brazilian modernismo and verdeamarelismo, Mexican estridentismo and the “Contemporáneos” group, and the impact in Latin America of surrealism and other European avant- garde movements. Readings in both Spanish and Portuguese, and the class is taught in both languages, but fluency in both languages is not expected.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6220 The Literature of Brazil (3)
Portuguese.description: In-depth study of Brazilian literature from its beginning to the present. Authors: Manuel Antônio de Almeida, José de Alencar, Gonçalves Dias, Castro Alves, Machado de Assis, Aluisio Azevedo, Graciliano Ramos, José Lins do Rêgo, Mário de Andrade, Oswald de Andrade, Manuel Bandeira, João Cabral de Melo Neto, Jorge Amado, Carlos Drummond de Andrade, Guimarães Rosa, Clarice Lispector, Antônio Callado, Lygia Fagundes Telles, Rubem Fonseca, Sérgio Sant'anna, Roberto Drummond, and others.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6230 Brazilian Lit & The City (3)
Portuguese.description: Brazilian literature and its production within an urban environment focusing of issues such as slavery and race relations, class divisions and spatial marginality, industrialization and labor movements, gender and sexuality, media and popular culture, rural to urban migration, and violence and  criminality. Authors may include Manuel Antônio de Almeida, Aluísio Azevedo, Machado de Assis, Lima Barreto, Mário de Andrade, Patricia Galvão, Marques Rebelo, Nelson Rodrigues, Rubem Fonseca, Caio Fernando Abreu, Patricia Melo, Paulo Lins, and Regina Rheda.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6290 Brazilian Cultural Studies (3)
Portuguese.description: An advanced survey of Brazilian social and cultural critics of the twentieth century including Silvio Romero, Euclides da Cunha, Gilberto Freyre, Sérgio Buarque de Hollanda, Guerrero Ramos, Roland Corbisier, Florestan Fernandes, Antônio Cândido, Roberto Schwarz, Ferreira Gullar, Silviano Santiago, Luiz Costa Lima, Flora Süssekind, Renato Ortiz, Muniz Sodré, and Marilena Chaui. The course foregrounds historic and contemporary debates in Brazil surrounding nationality, modernity, democracy, and citizenship.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6710 Contemp Fict Sp Am &Braz (3)
Portuguese.description: A comparison of the contemporary fiction of Spanish America and Brazil. Topics vary but may include: the short story; race, gender, and nationalism; the regionalist novel; experimental fiction; fiction and popular culture. Among the selected authors are Julio Cortázar, Guimarães Rosa, Fonseca, Borges, Clarice Lispector, Rulfo, Donoso, Icaza, Ramos, Rivera. Reading competence in Spanish and Portuguese to be established by previous course work or judgment of instructor.

Portuguese.name: PORT 6910 Special Topics (3-4)
Portuguese.description: Open to graduate students only.

Italian.name: ITAL 1010 Elementary Italian I (4)
Italian.description: Development of the skills of speaking, listening, reading, and writing Italian both in the classroom and through audio work.

Italian.name: ITAL 1020 Elementary Italian II (4)
Italian.description: A continuation of the objectives presented in Italian I.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 1020 Placement', ITAL 1010 or 1030 .

Italian.name: ITAL 1030 Elem Ital For Romnc Lang I (4)
Italian.description: Same material as ITAL 1010 but designed for students whose previous knowledge of another Romance language or Latin enables them to grasp the principles of Italian grammar and Italian vocabulary more efficiently. Development of the skills of speaking, understanding, reading, and writing Italian, both in the classroom and the language laboratory.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 1030 Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 1040 Elem Ital Romnc Lang II (4)
Italian.description: A continuation of the objectives of Italian for Romance Language Students I, with special emphasis on reading.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 1040 Placement', ITAL 1010 or 1030 .

Italian.name: ITAL 1050 Experiencing Rome: Conversational Language and Culture (3)
Italian.description: This course is designed for the Tulane Summer program, and it aims to provide students with the possibility to learn the Italian language while taking advantage of the fully immersive environment of the City of Rome.

Italian.description: Junior Semester.

Italian.description: A complete second-year course. Intensive grammar review with readings from standard Italian texts. Comprehension and conversational skills are stressed. Written expression also emphasized.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 2030 Placement', ITAL 1020 or 1040 .

Italian.description: A complete second-year course, covering the same material as ITAL 2030 , but designed for students whose previous knowledge of another Romance language or Latin enables them to grasp the principles of Italian grammar and Italian vocabulary more efficiently. Intensive grammar review with readings from standard Italian texts. Comprehension and conversational skills are stressed. Written expression also emphasized.

Italian.description:
Semester abroad.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Italian.name: ITAL 2940 Transfer Coursework (0-20)
Italian.description: Transfer Coursework at the 2000 level. Department approval may be required.

Italian.name: ITAL 3000 Introduction to Italian Literature (3)
Italian.description: An introduction to Italian literature, including readings from Dante, Petrarch, Boccaccio, Poliziano, Machiavelli, Ariosto, Castiglione, Goldoni, Manzoni, Pirandello, Calvino, among others.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3130 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3200 Placement', ITAL 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 or 4510 .

Italian.name: ITAL 3060 Roma Capitale d'Italia (3)
Italian.description: The course will focus on the central role and importance of Rome inside Italian culture and history along 150 years from the Italian Risorgimento (1871) to the present days. In particular, it will analyze specific social and cultural phenomena related to the 'Eternal City' such as the movie industry in the 1950s, the students' revolution in the '60s, the overpopulation and outgrowing of the outskirts in the '70s, the new symbol of the parasitic government in the '90s, and the new migration waves in the last 20 years. Lectures in English, reading and writing in Italian.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 3000 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4150, minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3130 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3250 Placement' or minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3200 Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 3130 Advanced Conversation and Composition (3)
Italian.description: The course aims primarily at perfecting the student's speaking and writing ability. Articles taken from newspapers, periodicals, the Internet, etc., serve as a basis for discussion and familiarize students with contemporary Italy. The course presupposes a solid grammatical foundation and any grammar review is given only on an individual basis.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 2030 , 3000 , 3060 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4150, minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3130 Placement', minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3250 Placement' or minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3200 Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 3200 Italian for business (3)
Italian.description: Italian for Business is a communicative course designed for students who wish to be prepared in professional areas related to international trade and organizations. It focuses on vocabulary, topics, and cultural aspects specific to an Italian-speaking professional environment. It reinforces the skills of listening, speaking, reading, and writing through the practice of simulated professional situations. The first part of the course will place more emphasis on the practice of communicative activities pertaining to professional functions. The second part of the course will place more emphasis on the student's understanding of company structures and operations. Taught in Italian.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 2030 , 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4510 , minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3130 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3250 Placement' or minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3200 Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 3250 Italian Language through History and Culture (3)
Italian.description: The course aims at improving the speaking and writing ability of students while familiarizing them with the development of Italian culture and history from pre-Roman era to the 21st century. Students discuss historical events and answer questions using the grammar and idioms learned in the grammar review. Writing assignments are based on the historical and cultural component of the course.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 3130 * , 3000 , 3060 , 3200 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4520 , minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3250 Placement', minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3130 Placement' or minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3200 Placement'. * May be taken concurrently.

Italian.name: ITAL 3300 Topics in Italian Literature and Cinema (3,4)
Italian.description:
Subject varies with instructor. An introductory study of the major contributions of Italian literature to Western thought. The course emphasizes particularly those authors whose works have interdisciplinary ramifications, e.g. Dante, Petrarch, Machiavelli, Galileo, Pirandello, Calvino. The course may also focus on the history of Italian cinema or a special topic in Italian cinema, such as the silent era, neorealism, the work of a major director, and the relationship between literature and film.
Notes: May be repeated for credit provided that a different topic is covered. The course counts for Film Studies credit only when the focus is on cinema.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4510 , minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3130 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3250 Placement' or minimum score of PASS in ' ITAL 3200 Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 3330 Ital Lit In Translation (3,4)
Italian.description: Subject varies with instructor. A study of the major contributions of Italian literature to Western thought. The course emphasizes particularly those authors whose works have interdisciplinary ramifications, e.g., Dante, Petrarch, Machiavelli, Galileo, Pirandello, Calvino. The course may also focus on the history of Italian cinema or a special topic in Italian cinema, such as the silent era, neorealism, the work of a major director, and the relationship between literature and film. Notes: May be repeated for credit, provided that a different topic is covered. The course counts for FMST credit only when the focus is on cinema.

Italian.name: ITAL 3360 Rome Capital of Italy (3)
Italian.description: The course will focus on the central role and importance of Rome inside Italian culture and history along 150 years from the Italian Risorgimento (1871) to the present days. In particular, it will analyze specific social and cultural phenomena related to the 'Eternal City' such as the movie industry in the 1950s, the students' revolution in the '60s, the overpopulation and outgrowing of the outskirts in the '70s, the new symbol of the parasitic government in the '90s, and the new migration waves in the last 20 years. Taught in English.

Italian.name: ITAL 3890 Service Learning (0-1)
Italian.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Italian.name: ITAL 3940 Transfer Coursework (0-20)
Italian.description: Transfer Coursework at the 3000 level. Department approval may be required.

Italian.name: ITAL 4010 Topics in 13th & 14th Century Italian Literature (3)
Italian.description: Topics may include St. Francis and early minor authors, Dante's Divine Comedy and early works, Boccaccio's Decameron and minor works, Petrarca's Canzoniere and minor works.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4510 or minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 4020 Topics in Renaissance Literature (3)
Italian.description: Topics may include the literati of the Medici court, lyric poetry of the Petrarchisti, the drama, the epic poem, political and social treatises.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4030 , 4040 or 4510 .

Italian.name: ITAL 4030 Topics in 17th & 18th Century Literature (3)
Italian.description:
Works of various literary and philosophical writers will be studied. Topics may include the effect of the Inquisition, the Petrarchan and Arcadian traditions, theater as social and political laboratory, Galileo, Bruno, Campanella, Marino, Vico, Metastasio, Gozzi, Goldoni, the beginnings of the Risorgimento in the works of such authors as Alfieri, Parini, and Foscolo.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4040 , 4510 or minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 4040 Topics in 19th & 20th Century Italian Literature (3,4)
Italian.description:
Topics may include Leopardi, Manzoni, Carducci, Verga, Pascoli, D'Annunzio, Pirandello, Calvino, Pasolini; the avant-garde; contemporary poetry, novel or drama; the history of Italian cinema and special topics in Italian cinema, such as the silent era, neorealism, the work of a major director, and the relationship between literature and film.
Notes: May be repeated for credit provided a different topic is covered.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 or 4510 .

Italian.name: ITAL 4440 Topics Lit/Cinema Transl (3,4)
Italian.description:
Subject varies with instructor. An advanced study of the major contributions of Italian literature to Western thought. The course emphasizes particularly those authors whose works have interdisciplinary ramifications, e.g. Dante, Petrarch, Machiavelli, Galileo, Pirandello, Calvino. The course may also focus on the history of Italian cinema or a special topic in Italian cinema, such as the silent era, neorealism, the work of a major director, and the relationship between literature and film. May be repeated for credit provided that a different topic is covered. Taught in English. Fulfills capstone requirement for FMST when the course is a film topic. For capstone credit, students should also register for FMST 5110 with 0 credits.
Notes: May be repeated for credit provided that a different topic is covered.

Italian.name: ITAL 4510 Narrazioni della migrazione in Italia: nuove face di una nazione (3,4)
Italian.description: The course provides an overview on the last thirty years of migration’s crisis in the Mediterranean Sea, with a focus on the immigration waves to Italy. Particular attention will be paid to the literary migrant voices.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement', ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 or 4040 .

Italian.name: ITAL 4515 Narratives of Migration in Italy: New Facets of a Nation (3,4)
Italian.description: The course provide an overview on the last thirty years of migration’s crisis in the Mediterranean Sea, with a particular focus on the immigration to Italy. In particular, it will focus on the literary voices of the migrants, while unpacking its social, political, anthropological contexts. Taught in English.

Italian.name: ITAL 4520 Cinema neorealista italiano (3-4)
Italian.description: A thorough study of Italian neorealist cinema, which at the end of WWII exposed Italy’s harsh social and conditions during the war and immediate postwar years. Moving across the movement's historical stages, from precursors in the 1930s, to ‘40s classics, to its legacy in the ‘50s and ‘60s, the course considers the term “neorealism” in its complexity, addressing its “myths,” affinities with traditions that it opposed such as Fascist cinema and Hollywood, and its modernist dimension. Films by Rossellini, De Sica, Visconti, De Santis, Fellini, Pasolini, among others.
Italian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ITAL 3000 , 3060 , 3130 , 3200 , 3250 , 3300 , 4010 , 4020 , 4030 , 4040 , 4510 or minimum score of PASS in 'ITAL 4000 Level Placement'.

Italian.name: ITAL 4525 Italian Neorealist Cinema (3-4)
Italian.description: A thorough study of Italian neorealist cinema, which at the end of WWII exposed Italy’s harsh social and conditions during the war and immediate postwar years. Moving across the movement's historical stages, from precursors in the 1930s, to ‘40s classics, to its legacy in the ‘50s and ‘60s, the course considers the term “neorealism” in its complexity, addressing its “myths,” affinities with traditions that it opposed such as Fascist cinema and Hollywood, and its modernist dimension. Films by Rossellini, De Sica, Visconti, De Santis, Fellini, Pasolini, among others. Taught in English.

Italian.name: ITAL 4560 Internship (1-2)
Italian.description:
An experiential learning process coupled with pertinent academic course work. Open only to juniors and seniors in good standing. Registration is completed in the academic department sponsoring the internship on TUTOR.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 1010 Elementary German I (4)
German.description: Development of basic language skills (listening, reading, speaking, and writing) with particular emphasis on the active use of present-day German. Cultural exploration of the German-speaking countries.

German.name: GERM 1020 Elementary German II (4)
German.description: Continuation of GERM 101. Continues the development of basic language skills (listening, reading, speaking, and writing) with particular emphasis on the active use of present-day German. Further exploration of the German-speaking countries.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' GERM 1020 Placement' or GERM 1010 .

German.description: In place of GERM 1010 and GERM 1020 . Accelerated development of basic language skills (listening, reading, speaking, and writing) with particular emphasis on the active use of present-day German. Cultural exploration of the German-speaking countries.

German.name: GERM 2030 Intermediate German (4)
German.description: Continues to develop proficiency in the four language skills (listening, reading, speaking, and writing) at the intermediate level. Further introduces students to contemporary German culture.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): GERM 1020 or minimum score of PASS in ' GERM 2030 Placement'.

German.name: GERM 2040 Intermed German II (4)
German.description: German 204 is the fourth semester of intermediate language study, following 203, and is the first required course for German majors and minors. Advanced practice in all discourse skills. Conducted entirely in German. Class discussion of readings, grammar review, composition, theatrical exercises. Student-driven communicative approach.

German.name: GERM 2390 Semester Abroad (1-20)

Semester Abroad.

German.name: GERM 2990 Foreign Language Exemption (0)
German.description: Foreign Language Exemption

German.name: GERM 3030 Intro To Literature (3)
German.description: Conducted in German. An introduction to representative works of prose, drama, and poetry of the German-speaking world.

German.name: GERM 3040 Intro To Literature (3)
German.description: Conducted in German. An introduction to representative works of prose, drama, and poetry of the German-speaking world (follows GERM 3030 ).

German.name: GERM 3050 Advanced Grammar & Composition (3)
German.description: Course combines language acquisition with content-based instruction for varying topics. With respect to language learning, the course aims at reinforcing and expanding students' proficiency primarily in writing.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' GERM 3050 Placement' or GERM 2030 .

German.name: GERM 3160 Readings In German Literature (3)
German.description: Conducted in German. Reading of representative works of German prose, drama, and poetry. Designed to introduce the student to fundamentals of literary analysis and to strategies for enhanced reading comprehension.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'GERM 3000 Level Placement' or GERM 3050 .

German.name: GERM 3250 German Lang & Culture I (3)
German.description: Conducted in German. Survey of German history from its beginning through the Age of Enlightenment, with emphasis on cultural and social aspects unique to Germany. Significant emphasis on the continued development of linguistic skills.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'GERM 3000 Level Placement' or GERM 3050 .

German.name: GERM 3260 German Lang & Culture II (3)
German.description: Conducted in German. Survey of German history from the end of the 18th century to the present including a discussion of institutions and problems of contemporary German life and civilization. Significant emphasis on the continued development of linguistic skills.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): GERM 3050 or minimum score of PASS in 'GERM 3000 Level Placement'.

German.name: GERM 3270 German Literature & Culture 1871-present (3)
German.description: This course traces significant events and developments in Germany from its beginning as a nation to its reunification at the end of the Cold War. Through close examination and discussion of selected literary, documentary, and filmic texts.
German.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'GERM 3000 Level Placement' or GERM 3050 .

German.name: GERM 3360 Translatn:Theory & Pract (3)
German.description: Proficiency in German required. Course introduces students to both practical and theoretical problems posed by translation in general and by English-German translation in particular. This class will learn by practicing translation and by reading theoretical texts about translation. Texts will include literature, news reports, and film subtitles.

German.name: GERM 3440 Representing Holocaust (3)
German.description: This course examines the Holocaust from various perspectives, disciplines, and media (including history, literature, and film) to investigate the conditions and limitations of representations of the Holocaust.

German.name: GERM 3510 Ger Cult & Civilization (3)
German.description: The emergence of art, music, and philosophy of the German-speaking peoples, primarily as reflected in their national literatures.

German.name: GERM 3530 Rehearsing Revolution (3)
German.description: The course examines major turning points in German history. How have German writers represented political revolutions and social upheavals from the French Revolution, the weavers' revolt of 1844, to the peaceful revolution of 1989? Conversely, to what extent has literature, especially drama, had an impact on revolutionary events? Authors and theorists considered include such classics as Goethe, Schiller, Kleist, Büchner , Marx, Hannah Arendt, Brecht, Müller , and Weiss. Films by Riefenstahl, Fassbinder, and Becker.

German.name: GERM 3540 Marx, Nietzsche, & Freud (3)
German.description: Course introduces three philosophical revolutionaries who have exerted enormous influence on literature, philosophy, psychology, and politics. With its intellectual-historical approach, the course will examine key terms and analytical models in these thinkers as well as the intersection points among them.

German.name: GERM 3550 Germ Lit In Translation (3)
German.description:
Subject varies and is announced each semester. Typically a study of literary movements, genres, individual authors, or themes, e.g., the treatment of the Faust theme in German literature.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 3560 The Devil's Pact (3)
German.description: The Devil's Pact in Literature, Film, Music.

German.name: GERM 3660 Love, Death & Sexuality (3)
German.description: The focus of this course will be the representation of love, death and sexuality in German culture from the Middle Ages to the Thirty Years War. Selected works of literature, music and art will be examined.

German.name: GERM 3670 Grimm: Devel German Fairy Tale (3)
German.description: This course will examine the Brothers Grimm and the classic fairy tales: their origins, development and later adaptations (and will include tales from other cultures and traditions as well).

German.name: GERM 3710 Intro To German Film (3)
German.description: This course explores the trajectory of German film from its Expressionist beginnings to the present. How do the narratives presented aid in understanding the specific historical, social, cultural, and political moments in which they were produced?

German.name: GERM 3720 Weimar Cinema (3)
German.description: This course traces the development of the filmic production of Weimar Germany, as well as its influence on classic and contemporary Hollywood film noir. Analyzing significant films from the era, we trace the stylistic, generic, and thematic trends.

German.name: GERM 3730 Nazi Cinema (3)
German.description: Nazi Cinema and Nazis in Cinema: Fascist Imaginary, Imagined Fascists.

German.name: GERM 3890 Service Learning (0-1)
German.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 3891 Service Learning (0-1)
German.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 4250 Adv Comp Convr Phonetics (3)
German.description: Advanced Composition, Conversation, and Phonetics for advanced German students.

Advanced Undergraduate Seminar on varying topics. For course description, consult department.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 4410 The German Novelle (3)
German.description: Study of Novellas by Goethe, Kleist, Arnim, E.T.A. Hoffmann, Gotthelf, Droste-Hü lshoff, Keller, Storm, Hauptmann, Hofmannsthal, Zweig, and Th. Mann, illustrating the historical development of the German Novella as a literary form.

German.name: GERM 4430 German Drama (3)
German.description: A study of the German dramatic tradition through close analysis of representative plays by such writers as Lessing, Schiller, Goethe, Kleist, Hebbel, Grillparzer, and Bü chner.

German.name: GERM 4490 Shorter Forms Ger Prose (3)
German.description: Shorter forms of German prose, including essays and short stories from different time periods

German.name: GERM 4710 Special Topics (3,4)
German.description:
Special Topics in German Literature. Consult course schedule for current topic.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 4720 Special Topics (3)
German.description: Special Topics in German. For description, consult department.

German.name: GERM 4800 Advanced Undergrad Sem (3)
German.description:
Topics vary from year to year. Typically an intensive study of an individual writer, a limited genre, a literary movement or a thematic problem. The Experience of War; Germany's Roaring 1920s; German Culture after WW II; Youth and the German Nation; Post-1989 Literary and Visual Culture; Travels to Foreign Lands; Early Modern Maps and Images.
Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.name: GERM 4801 Orient und Okzident:  Interkulturelle Begegnungen Im Mittelalter Und Der Frühen Neuzeit (3)
German.description: This course will examine medieval and early modern travel accounts in the German-speaking world from ca. 1200-1800 CE. Using a blend of primary and secondary sources in literature (including journals, theater, poetry) and visual sources (maps, costume books, material objects) we will explore the concepts of “East” and “West” and the development of stereotypes in the representation of foreign peoples and places.

German.name: GERM 4810 Special Topics Germn Lit (3)

German.description: Special Topics in German literature. For description, consult department. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

German.description:

to read German journalistic and academic prose. No prior knowledge of German is required. The only prerequisite is complete competence in English grammar and syntax.

Russian.name: RUSS 1010 Elementary Russian I (4)
Russian.description: Introduction to Russian grammar. Development of basic language skills, with particular emphasis on the active use of present-day Russian. For students with little or no knowledge of Russian. Meets four times a week.

Russian.description: Continuation of the development of introductory language skills. Practice in reading, speaking, writing and understanding.
Russian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' RUSS 1020 Placement' or RUSS 1010 .

Russian.description: Advancement of all language skills, including aural-oral, writing, and communicative fluency. Reading of literary texts.
Russian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' RUSS 2030 Placement' or RUSS 1020 .

Russian.description: This course is a continuation of second-year Russian. Discussion of and essays on subjects related to Russian history, culture, and contemporary life. Advancement of all language skills.

Russian.name: RUSS 3030 Masterpieces Russ Lit I (3)
Russian.description: Selected readings from among the most outstanding works of Russian literature from its beginnings through the 19th century. Advancement of all language skills through study and analysis of literary texts.

Russian.name: RUSS 3040 Masterpieces Russ Lit II (3)
Russian.description: Selected readings from the most outstanding works of 20th-century Russian literature. Advancement of all language skills through study and analysis of literary texts.

Russian.name: RUSS 3250 Advanced Russian Grammar (3)
Russian.description: Phonemic, grammatical, and syntactical patterns of standard literary Russian. Practice in composition and vocabulary building.

Russian.name: RUSS 3330 Oral Discussion (3)
Russian.description: Discussion of topics from contemporary Russian politics and history. Students learn syntax and vocabulary aimed at building discourse competence. Hypothesization, narration, questioning, contradicting, speaking in paragraphs. Reading, listening, speaking.

Russian.name: RUSS 3450 Tolstoy/Dostoevsky-Trans (3)
Russian.description: Readings and discussions of the major novels. Comparative study of literary method, theme and structure, modern critical approaches. No knowledge of Russian required. May be counted toward major.

Russian.name: RUSS 3530 Survey of Russian Art (3)
Russian.description: An introduction to the art and architecture of Russia, from the 12th century to the present. The first part of the course deals with the medieval period (church architecture, icons, frescoes). The second part begins with the assimilation of western Europe.

Russian.name: RUSS 3700 Russian Poetry (3)
Russian.description: Readings in Russian poetry, including Pushkin, Lermontov, Tyutchev, and the symbolists. Lectures, discussions, and compositions in Russian.
Russian.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): RUSS 2040 or minimum score of PASS in 'RUSS 3000 Level Placement'.

Russian.name: RUSS 3780 Soviet Jewish Experience (3,4)
Russian.description: The course examines major trends in Russian Jewish history, culture and identity as developed during the Soviet period.  Taught in English.

Arabic.description: Basic introduction to the Arabic language. Emphasis on listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

Arabic.name: ARBC 1020 Elementary Arabic II (4)
Arabic.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ARBC 1010 or minimum score of PASS in ' ARBC 1020 Placement'.

Arabic.name: ARBC 1290 Junior Year Abroad (1-20)
Arabic.description: Junior year abroad. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Arabic.name: ARBC 2030 Intermediate Arabic (4)
Arabic.description: Third semester of Arabic language. Continues development of reading and writing Arabic, but emphasis is placed on oral performance.
Arabic.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ARBC 2030 Placement' or ARBC 1020 .

Arabic.name: ARBC 2910 Independent Study (1-3)
Arabic.description: Independent study in Arabic.Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Arabic.name: ARBC 3010 Special Topics (3)
Arabic.description: Special topics in language, literature, and culture of the Arab world.  Course may be repeated 3 times for credit.

Arabic.name: ARBC 3060 Business Arabic (3)
Arabic.description: Introduction to the vocabulary and topics related to business in the Arabic speaking world.

Arabic.name: ARBC 3150 Advanced Arabic (3)
Arabic.description: A continuation of skills developed in ARBC 2030 , which is a prerequisite for ARBC 3150 .  The Arabic language is used as the medium of instruction, and in addition to the further development of reading, writing, listening, and speaking skills, a greater emphasis is placed on culture.
Arabic.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ARBC 3150 Placement' or ARBC 2030 .

Arabic.name: ARBC 3170 Media Arabic (3)
Arabic.description: This course will introduce students to vocabulary and topics related to contemporary Arabic media.

Arabic.name: ARBC 3220 Introduction to Arabic Literature (3)
Arabic.description: A general survey of modern Arabic literature. Major trends and genres such as romanticism and realism. Focus on modern Arabic fiction, especially post-colonial period. Sample authors studied include, among others: Hanan Al-Shaykh, Baha’ Tahir.
Arabic.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'ARBC 3000 Placement', minimum score of PASS in ' ARBC 3150 Placement' or ARBC 3150 .

Arabic.name: ARBC 3250 Arab Modern Culture (3)
Arabic.description: Intensive language and culture training and a "hands on" learning experience. May combine academic instruction on Contemporary Arab social and cultural issues with community service focusing on critical, reflective thinking and personal and civic responsibility.

Arabic.name: ARBC 3300 Arabic for International Relations and Diplomacy (3)
Arabic.description: Introduction to vocabulary and topics related to contemporary politics, foreign service, international relations and diplomacy through Arabic media. Lexical knowledge and structures related to the topics above through authentic materials, including broadcast media.

Arabic.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 1010 Beginning Chinese I (4)
Chinese Language.description: Designed for students to acquire a knowledge of the fundamentals of the Chinese language to be demonstrated in four areas of basic language skills: oral and listening comprehension, speaking, writing (Chinese characters), and some reading ability.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 1020 Beginning Chinese II (4)
Chinese Language.description: A continuation of the objectives presented in Beginning Chinese I. Attention is given to practical and topics-oriented conversational skills, moods of speech, and complex level of syntax.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 1890 Service Learning (0-1)
Chinese Language.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 1940 Transfer Coursework (0-20)
Chinese Language.description: Transfer Coursework at the 1000 level. Departmental approval may be required.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 2030 Intermediate Chinese I (4)
Chinese Language.description: An intensive study of communicative Chinese in both speaking and writing on daily topics. Continued emphasis on reading, writing, listening, and speaking abilities.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 2040 Intermediate Chinese II (4)
Chinese Language.description: A continuation of intensive study of communicative Chinese in both speaking and writing with greater length and more diverse topics. Continued emphasis on reading, writing, listening, and speaking abilities.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 2890 Service Learning (0-1)
Chinese Language.description: Students complete a service activity in the community in conjunction with the content of a three-credit co-requisite course. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 2990 Foreign Language Exempt (0)
Chinese Language.description: Foreign language exempt.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3050 Adv Chinese Language I (4)
Chinese Language.description: First semester advanced instruction in the Chinese language, including conversation, reading, and writing.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3051 Heritage Chinese (4)
Chinese Language.description: ASTC 3051 is tailored to accommodate students who grew up in an environment in which Chinese is spoken by parents or guardians at home and for those who are familiar with the language and possess intermediate-mid proficiency level in listening comprehension and speaking. Students will be listening to and read about topics about their surroundings, such as colors and shapes, forms of objects, types of products, materials and specifications, physical features of a person, apparels and attires, industry and professions, nature and disaster, geographical environment and natural resources, city and urban characteristics etc.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3060 Adv Chinese Language II (4)
Chinese Language.description: An advanced course with emphasis on language functions (making comparison, detailed description, reporting, etc.) This course aims to continue improving students’ Chinese communicative and presentational competence.
Chinese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ASTC 3060 Placement' or ASTC 3050 .

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3065 Issues in Contemporary China (4)
Chinese Language.description: An advanced course aimed to develop students’ advanced language skills in both spoken and written forms as well as knowledge of contemporary Chinese culture and society. Students are expected to listen, watch and read multi-media materials in Chinese about modern Chinese society.
Chinese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ASTC 3060 or minimum score of PASS in ' ASTC 3065 Placement'.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3070 Business Chinese (3)
Chinese Language.description: This course is for students who have achieved intermediate-low/mid Chinese language proficiency. The goal is for students to acquire the necessary linguistic competence and understand the business culture in China to function effectively in a business setting.  At the end of the semester, students are expected to the proficiency level of intermediate-mid to advanced low in listening, speaking, reading and writing (based on ACTFL foreign language proficiency guidelines).

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3890 Service Learning (0-1)
Chinese Language.description: Service Learning

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 3910 Special Topics (1-3)
Chinese Language.description: Special topics in Asian studies.  Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 4070 Adv Chinese Read & Write (3)
Chinese Language.description: The course aims to improve students' proficiency in Mandarin Chinese at advanced level as well as knowledge of Chinese culture and society.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 4080 Chinese Media Literacy (3)
Chinese Language.description: An advanced course that focuses on developing students' skills in Mandarin Chinese as well as knowledge of contemporary Chinese culture and society. Students will be listening to, watching or reading multi-media materials from the media on modern Chinese society.

Chinese Language.name: ASTC 4350 Chinese Lit and Culture (3)
Chinese Language.description: This course aims at improving students reading and writing proficiency in the language through the study of the writings of Chinese authors. Students will read, discuss, and write about essays written in the contemporary period.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 1010 Beginning Japanese I (4)
Japanese Language.description: Emphasizes conversational Japanese. Includes study of basic grammar and introduction of hiragana, and katakana.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 1020 Beginning Japanese II (4)
Japanese Language.description: Emphasizes conversational Japanese based on text in hiragana, katakana, kanji. Includes study of complex grammar and introduction of approximately 100 kanji.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ASTJ 1020 Placement' or ASTJ 1010 .

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 2030 Intermediate Japanese I (4)
Japanese Language.description: Conversation, reading, and writing based on text in hiragana, katakana and kanji. Continuation of study of complex grammar and introduction of approximately 100 additional kanji.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ASTJ 2030 Placement' or ASTJ 1020 .

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 2040 Intermediate Japanese II (4)
Japanese Language.description: Conversation, reading, and writing based on text in hiragana, katakana, and kanji. Continuation of study of complex grammar and introduction of approximately 150 additional kanji.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in ' ASTJ 2040 Placement' or ASTJ 2030 .

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 3050 Advanced Japanese Speaking I (3)
Japanese Language.description: Development of conversational, reading and writing skills in Japanese.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ASTJ 2040 or minimum score of PASS in ' ASTJ 3050 Placement'.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 3051 Advanced Japanese Speaking II (3)
Japanese Language.description: ASTJ 3051 is a continuation of ASTJ 3050 , reinforcing previously learned grammar points as well as deepening knowledge of Japanese culture and society through discussion and reading.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ASTJ 3050 or minimum score of PASS in ' ASTJ 3051 Placement'.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 3060 Advanced Japanese Reading II (3)
Japanese Language.description: A continuation of objectives in ASTJ 2040 , intended to provide an advanced level of skills in Japanese.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 4060 Advanced Japanese Comp & Pres (3)
Japanese Language.description: This is a fourth-year advanced Japanese composition and presentation course that will help students develop their vocabulary, writing, and oral presentation skills. The students will learn about effective communication strategies in different types of linguistic situations. The course will also expose students to various aspects of Japanese culture and society.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): minimum score of PASS in 'ASTJ 4000 Level Placement' or ASTJ 3051 .

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 4070 Advanced Japanese Composition & Presentation II (3)
Japanese Language.description: This class is a continuation of its prerequisite, ASTJ 4060 . It is designed to equip the students with an ability to participate in Japanese conversation, exchange information and express feelings. The course also aims at deepening an understanding and developing appreciation for Japanese tradition as well as for the students' own culture.
Japanese Language.prerequisites_raw: Prerequisite(s): ASTJ 4060 or minimum score of PASS in 'ASTJ 4000 Level Placement'.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 4910 Independent Study (1-3)
Japanese Language.description: Independent study in Japanese language. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.

Japanese Language.name: ASTJ 5190 Semester Abroad (1-20)
Japanese Language.description: Semester abroad in Asian studies. Course may be repeated up to unlimited credit hours.
